data.title: Essential Utilities: Not Too Late To Buy Now
data.description:
As a quality-oriented investor, I’m a fan of companies with consistent track records of dividend growth. In an era of rapid market shifts and high-interest rates, the Dividend Champions offer some intriguing options, in my opinion. These are publicly traded companies with at least 25 years of payout raises to their credit.

Within this elite group of dividend payers, regulated water utilities have carved out a unique niche. They tend to offer a mix of high barriers to entry (it’s very expensive to build out an extensive infrastructure network), reliable demand for utility services, and predictable regulatory frameworks.

That leads me into my focus for today, which is Essential Utilities (WTRG). When I last covered WTRG with a Buy rating in January, I covered it from the angle of what it would look like if its pending merger with American Water Works (AWK) closed. I was encouraged by an improvement in the EPS growth profile from 5% to 7% independently to 7% to 9% in such a case. A one-notch improvement in the S&P credit rating to A was another positive. In closing, both regulated water utilities were undervalued.

A couple of months later, I’m going to lean more toward the deal not being completed; the odds are probably two or three to one that it will be cleared. One big step in this direction was that in February, 99% of AWK’s shares voted to approve the deal, and 95% of WTRG’s voted in support of it. It still must win over state regulators, so it’s not a done deal yet.

On its own, WTRG is poised to keep generating 5% to 7% annual adjusted EPS growth. The company’s A- S&P credit rating keeps its cost of capital low. That allows it to bridge the capex that’s not covered by operating cash flow and gives it the capital needed for accretive bolt-on acquisitions. Clinching my continued Buy rating, shares are still priced at a double-digit percentage discount to my fair value estimate.

Steady Business Growth Ahead

On February 25th, WTRG released its earnings report for the fourth quarter ended December 31st, 2025. The company’s total operating revenue rose by 15.7% year-over-year to $699.1 million during the quarter.

What contributed to WTRG’s double-digit percentage topline growth rate in the fourth quarter? The biggest driver for the utility for the quarter was the successful execution of its regulatory strategy. Throughout 2025, WTRG secured rate awards and surcharges across both the Water and Natural Gas segments.

The former segment recorded $329.4 million in operating revenue during the fourth quarter, which was up 8.0% over the year-ago period. Increases in water and wastewater rates were the primary contributing factor to these results.

The latter segment reported $361.3 million in operating revenue in the fourth quarter, which was a 23% year-over-year growth rate. Rate case successes here and specialized surcharges like the Infrastructure Improvement Charge were instrumental to these results. Favorable weather also benefited the Natural Gas segment. The final component of the segment’s outsized topline growth was higher purchased gas costs. As a regulated utility, WTRG passes these costs onto customers without a markup. While this is neutral to net income, it artificially boosts the topline in times of higher commodity prices.

Shifting to the bottom line, WTRG recorded $0.47 in diluted EPS for the fourth quarter. That was down 29.9% over the year-ago period. This beat Seeking Alpha’s analyst consensus during the quarter by $0.07. Rather than anything concerning, the reason for the drop in net income had to do with the absence of a one-time gain from asset sales that occurred in Q4 2024. Higher operations and maintenance expenses from a larger rate base played a role as well.

Moving forward, WTRG has plenty of growth tailwinds.

For one, there’s the fact that the regulated utility is planning to invest $1.7 billion in 2026. That would be an acceleration from the record $1.4 billion allocated in 2025. This will be used to replace aging water mains and gas pipelines, PFAS remediation, and accelerate the rollout of Advanced Metering Infrastructure and AI-driven predictive maintenance to enhance billing accuracy and operational efficiency. This will drive further rate base growth for WTRG, which will propel operating revenue higher over time.

Additionally, the company has $101.9 million of requested annualized revenue increases pending via rate cases in North Carolina, Texas, Ohio, Virginia, and New Jersey. The company has already had $12.4 million of annualized revenue increases from other rate cases that went into effect in January.

One final tailwind for WTRG is its A- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook. This low cost of capital allowed it to issue $500 million of 10-year senior notes at 5.125% in March. In this rate environment, those are attractive terms. This is important because it helps the company to execute bolt-on acquisitions of water and wastewater systems in its service territories (acquisitions amounting to $300 million and over 200,000 customers are currently pending in various transactions for WTRG).

This is why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted diluted EPS to grow by 1.8% in 2026 to $2.24. For 2027, another 7.1% jump in adjusted diluted EPS to $2.40 is anticipated. In 2028, an additional 5% increase in adjusted diluted EPS is being projected (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to WTRG’s Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and WTRG’s Q4 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Solid Upside To Fair Value

Since January, WTRG’s shares have logged a 5% total return. That convincingly beat the S&P 500 index’s (SP500) -6% total return over the period. Even so, there’s still value to be had with WTRG right now.

The stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 17.9. That’s up only slightly from the prior value of 17.7. For more perspective, this is around three standard deviations less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 25.2 (down slightly from 25.6 as the higher valuation reference year of 2015 was replaced with the lower valuation reference year of 2025), according to FAST Graphs.

Moving forward, I would argue that a fair value P/E ratio for WTRG remains right around two standard deviations below the 10-year average multiple. Independently, the company’s 4.9% annual forward adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus is about the same as its 10-year CAGR of 5.7%. That supports my argument that WTRG’s growth prospects are intact.

Just like in January, though, the current interest rate environment is positioned to remain a major drag on the water utility’s fair value P/E ratio. The current 4.3% 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) is much higher than the 10-year average yield of roughly 2.7%, per Macrotrends.

I continue to think that the 10-year yield will consistently be in the low-4% range in the coming years. Applying a roughly 100 basis point spread to WTRG’s earnings yield relative to the 10-year yield (similar to the 10-year average spread of 100 to 125 basis points), this is how I arrive at a fair value multiple of 20.

As things stand, the calendar year 2026 is 25% complete. That suggests there’s another 75% of 2026 and 25% of 2027 yet to come over the next 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input of $2.28.

Plugging that into my fair value P/E ratio of 20, I compute a fair value of $46 a share. Compared to the current $41 share price (as of April 2nd, 2026), that equates to a 10% discount to fair value.

If WTRG meets growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could generate a 17% total return by the end of March 2027. Through 2031, the water utility could put up 10% annual total returns. If its deal with AWK is ultimately completed, the return potential would be about the same (higher growth prospects would compensate for the lower yield).

The Payout Can Keep Rising

WTRG’s 3.4% forward dividend yield comes in about 20 basis points above the 3.2% median forward yield of the utilities sector. That represents a shift from January, in which the two were at parity with each other. This explains how WTRG earns B- and C+ grades from Seeking Alpha’s Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (both of which are the same as they were in January).

The company’s dividend is also reasonably sustainable. This is evidenced by a payout ratio that’s likely to be in the low-60% range in 2026. According to the Zen Research Terminal, that would be comfortably less than the 75% payout ratio set forth by rating agencies as the upper-limit for the industry. This would also be perfectly within its targeted payout ratio range of between 60% and 65%.

As a result, the Zen Research Terminal pegs the risk of a dividend cut as an independent company at only 0.7% within the next 12 months. In the next average recession, that risk rises to 2%. It’s again worth noting that in the likely event that WTRG’s merger with AWK is approved, the dividend will be cut by about 26%, according to the math from fellow SA analyst Ian Bezek.

Should the deal fall through, that sets up WTRG to build on its 34-year dividend growth streak. This is already almost quadruple the sector median of 8.9 years, which is enough to garner an A grade from the Quant System for the consecutive years of dividend growth metric.

The Quant System is projecting 5.7% annual forward dividend per share growth for WTRG. This would be above the sector median of 5.4%. In my view, that’s a realistic expectation for dividend growth since the payout ratio can allow WTRG to grow the dividend in line with adjusted diluted EPS for the foreseeable future. This accounts for the Quant System’s B grade for the metric.

Risks To Consider

In either form, WTRG is a regulated utility with a clear path to growth in the years ahead. Still, it faces risks that should be watched in the future.

WTRG and AWK decisively approved the merger in February. However, the deal isn’t done yet. The next regulatory hurdles for the pending deal to clear are approvals from state public utility commissions, including Pennsylvania and Illinois.

As I noted in January, the tie-up of the nation’s two biggest utilities won’t come without its share of controversy. In many jurisdictions, WTRG and AWK often compete against each other in buyouts of local assets, such as in Chester, Pennsylvania, with the city’s local sewage facility up for sale. Combining these two will reduce competition for government-owned utility buyouts, which could result in better deals for a combined entity.

That’s precisely the risk that could hold this deal back, though. In exchange for giving the deal the green light, state regulators will likely require concessions such as potential rate freezes or mandating a portion of merger savings be passed directly to customers.

If the merger is complete, the combined company would still have to navigate the integration phase. This carries the risk of customer service disruptions and important personnel leaving the company.

Another risk is the refinancing of older, cheaper debt with more expensive debt in this higher-rate environment. In an environment where more Americans are concerned about higher utility bills, it could be a delicate needle for WTRG to thread to recover these higher financing costs in future rate cases.

Summary: A Quality Utility With Decent Return Potential

WTRG is positioned to keep growing at a respectable rate regardless of whether it becomes part of AWK. The water utility’s continued infrastructure investments and room for more bolt-on acquisitions bode well for the future. As does its A- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook. The payout has room to keep growing in line with earnings growth. Lastly, shares remain moderately undervalued. That’s why I’m standing by my Buy rating.

data.title: NiSource: Positioned For Further Data Center-Powered Growth
data.description:
Shares of NiSource (NI) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 17%. In my view, this utility is one of the best positioned to benefit from growing data center electricity usage. While there has been political pushback against data centers over fears of rising electricity costs, NiSource has created structures to isolate these costs, leaving it well positioned to weather any political challenge. Investors appear to be recognizing these strengths, with shares up 13% since I rated them a “buy” in January, even as the broader market has declined. With shares having passed my $45 price target and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit NI to determine if shares remain a buy or if investors should take profits.

Importantly, in its March investor update, NI reiterated its 8-9% EPS growth target through 2033, though growth will be a bit faster after 2030 as it enjoys the full benefits of its AMZN project. This assumes a 9-11% rate base growth, and there will be a 1-3% headwind from financing costs as NI will be an ongoing issuer of debt and equity to fund a portion of its capital program.

While past success does not guarantee future results, it is worth noting that NI has consistently met or exceeded previous years’ outlooks. This has come without even realizing the benefits of data center demand, which is largely a 2026-2035 phenomenon as these massive projects are built out. NiSource’s ability to deliver and exceed guidance is driven, in my view, by the company’s focus on cap-ex that is recovered within 12 months, providing a clearer line of sight to revenue and less regulatory risk. Because its cap-ex spending is still largely centered around such efforts, I believe the company is positioned to deliver on its targets.

This solid performance continued in 2025; NiSource earned $1.90 in adjusted EPS with Q4 coming in at $0.51 thanks to benefits of regulatory rate capture. I would also note that the company benefited from a cold start to the winter, boosting operating income by $12 million in the quarter and $33 million in the calendar year. This was a nice improvement from 2024 when weather was a $60 million headwind. Weather can be volatile year-to-year but should average out over time.

Looking forward, over the next five years, it will be investing $28 billion to improve and modernize its infrastructure. There is $21 billion of baseline spending, with $6-7 billion for the Amazon project. Under its agreement with Amazon (AMZN), which isolates out the cost of generating electricity for its data center, customers should receive about $1 billion in cost savings or a peak of $7-9 per month off their bill. I expect the Amazon contract to receive regulatory approval by June, with the proceeding progressing as hoped, including a settlement in principle that is consistent with the original arrangement.

As you can see below, the majority of its spending is tied to projects where it gets recovery within 6 months, and 80-90% comes from projects where it recovers the cost within a year. Potential data center deals create long-term upside to cap-ex and rate base growth. However, given the timing of such projects, they are likely to be several years away. More data centers will keep growth higher in the 2030s but do little to shift the next 2-3 years. Increasingly, I am focused on Ohio as a state with meaningful opportunity.

Ohio has taken legislative action to spur development of data centers in the state. Under the new law, large customers like data center can sign bespoke agreements with utility companies for their power, essentially isolating their cost from the rest of the grid. This will allow NiSource’s Ohio utility to engage in the same type of contract its Indiana subsidiary did with Amazon. From a political perspective, this is a very attractive option for state leaders. Given the political toxicity of inflation, with electricity bills already having surged (see below), there is fear of approving data centers and having higher customer bills. That said, states want the construction jobs and investment benefits of these large projects.

These deals give them the best of both worlds with investment in their state plus confidence that customer bills will not be impacted, muting the political risk of inflation. This also has become an acceptable solution for big tech companies. Given the intensity of the race in AI, getting quicker approval is essential, which means solving potential political objections over cost. Plus, so long as they actually use the electricity they anticipate, they ultimately will not bear financial risk. With companies like Amazon and Meta (META) striking such deals across different states, I expect this effort to “isolate” data center costs to be the go-forward norm. This helps de-risk the utilities’ cap-ex plans and is a positive for companies like NiSource.

Critically, NiSource maintains a sound balance sheet, which enables it to undertake this massive capital program and insulates it better from a period of higher-for-longer interest rates. FFO to debt is 16.1%, at the top end of its 14-16% target. As you can see below, the company will be consistently issuing equity to support its cap-ex spending and maintain solid balance sheet metrics. I expect ~$400 million of annual equity issuance under its baseline plan.

This points to about ~1.75% annual dilution. The good news is that the higher NI goes, the fewer shares it needs to issue in order to raise the same amount of capital. As such, the strength of its stock has helped to essentially reduce the dilution cost of its cap-ex program, boosting long-term earnings growth vs. if shares were still below $40. Now, if more data center deals are signed, I expect its equity issuance needs to rise. As a general rule of thumb, each $1 billion of incremental cap-ex is likely to require another ~$200 million of equity issuance.

Shares offer a 2.5% dividend yield, and NiSource has a strong track record as a dividend payer with 14 straight years of growth. Over the past five years, its dividend has grown at a 6% pace. With a solid balance sheet and credible, significant cap-ex program, I view NI as likely to continue growing its dividend, likely by 6-8% over the next 5 years. The company is targeting $2.02-$2.07 in EPS this year. Given its highly regulated nature, I view guidance as highly credible. This winter was abnormally cold in the eastern half of the United States, and that should support higher residential demand in Q1. As such, I am targeting $2.05-$2.10 of EPS this year.

Back in January, I saw the company growing earnings 8-8.5% over the next five years with another data center deal necessary to push growth past 9%. I still expect 8+% growth, and I am cautiously optimistic about future deals with data centers given efforts in Ohio and Pennsylvania to compete for these projects. With ~6-8% dividend growth and a 2.5% starting yield. I see shares likely to generate a long-term return of 8.5-10.5%.

This puts shares right around my ~10% buy threshold, leaving it between a “buy” and a “hold.” While not as compelling as three months ago, I am maintaining NI at a “buy” as I view it as having one of the most favorable regulatory environments and a clear structure to enable data center investment. Especially in this uncertain macro environment, NI’s regulated cash flows are attractive, and I see shares returning about 10% over the next year.

data.title: Semiconductors Winners And Losers At The Start Of Q2 2026
data.description:
The semiconductor sector got off to a great start, but it lost most of the gains by the end of Q1 2026 as the sector stumbled towards the latter part of the quarter. Semiconductor stocks were able to rally higher on the back of a number of tailwinds, but semis got stopped in their tracks as headwinds arose, especially on the geopolitical front. Individual stocks may be able to go against the grain, but Q2 2026 is likely to see more downwards pressure on stocks, unless a solution is found to what is weighing on the sector.

Tailwinds have morphed into headwinds for semis

A previous article at the start of Q1 2026 pointed out how semiconductor stocks look poised for gains because they had a number of powerful tailwinds in their favor, which included accommodation in terms of Fed monetary policy, but, at the same time, these tailwinds had the potential to turn into headwinds. In the end, the article concluded the sector was probably heading higher, but only if tailwinds like Fed policy and AI remained in its corner throughout the whole year.

These comments at the start of 2026 have begun to look rather prescient with one quarter done. For instance, the iShares PHLX Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), an ETF which tracks the semiconductor space, wound up with a gain of 9.13% in Q1 2026, which allowed it to outperform both the SPDR S&P500 ETF (SPY) with a loss of 4.63% and the Invesco QQQ trust (QQQ) with a loss of 6.04%.

However, SOXX was up as much as 22.47% at one point in Q1 2026 and the main reason why it lost most of the quarter’s gains was because geopolitical tensions in the Middle East turned into a full-blown regional war as the U.S. and Iran came to blows at the end of February. As stated in the article at the start of the year, semis look poised to benefit from monetary easing from the Federal Reserve, which included not just lower interest rates, but also the resumption in the buying of Treasury Bills.

However, the war in Iran and the restrictions imposed on the Strait of Hormuz have thrown a spanner into the works by potentially derailing the ability of the Fed to ease since doing so could further inflame inflation, and by extension the cost of living many people are complaining about. It even has the potential of forcing the Fed to do a one-eighty by resorting to monetary tightening to curb rising inflation, which has in past history given growth stocks, which includes semis, all sorts of problems.

Why Q1 2026 could have been worse

It is worth mentioning that while semis as a whole ended up despite losses in March, the final results could have been worse if certain things had gone a little bit differently. For instance, the chart posted earlier shows how SOXX rallied higher since it hit bottom in April 2025, which was the result of a selloff that hit stocks following tariffs and “Liberation Day”.

In fact, not only did SOXX rally higher, but it appears to have followed a trendline higher for the past 12 months or so. Yet there were several instances in which SOXX came close to breaking the trendline by moving below it, including twice in the month of March. For instance, SOXX hit an intraday low of $316.22 on March 9, only to end the day much higher at $336.37.

This is significant because if we retrace what SOXX did beforehand, which saw SOXX rally from the April 2025 low of $148.31 to the February 2026 high of $368.82, as shown in the earlier chart, then the first potential support level or the 23.6% Fibonacci retracement of $148.31 to $368.82 is $316.78, or less than one percent, $0.56 to be exact, removed from $316.22, the March 9 low. In other words, SOXX had started to peak below a support level, which could have led to a break of support, but the timely bounce in the stock allowed it to clear the first challenge by showing that support is standing its ground.

Another challenge occurred later in March, March 30 to be more specific, which saw SOXX hit an intraday low of $307.26, or well below the previously mentioned support at $316.78, although it closed the day somewhat higher at $309.79. This is the lowest the stock has closed at in 2026, which puts it not only below the support provided by the Fibonacci retracement level mentioned earlier, but also below the uptrend, as defined by the trendline in the chart posted, that has been in place since April of last year.

However, another bounce the next day or March 31 allowed SOXX to get back above both the trendline and support provided by a Fibonacci retracement because the stock closed at $328.66 that day, or well above $316.78. More importantly, it is worth mentioning that both of these two dates, March 9 and March 31, which saw SOXX rally 4% and 6%, respectively, on these dates, coincided with major statements from President Trump.

March 31 saw President Trump announce the war with Iran could end in 2-3 weeks as the U.S. would be leaving soon. This is similar to what happened on March 9 when President Trump stated during a press conference that the war with Iran was a short-term excursion that could be finished pretty quickly. The market likes the idea of an imminent end to the war in the Middle East and the market got what it wanted to hear on both dates, which caused stocks to rally, SOXX included.

It is of course possible that it is a coincidence for these statements to have occurred on dates when stocks like SOXX were at a critical junction, close to breaking through support, which likely would have paved the way for more downside. On the other hand, if these events had not occurred, at a specific point on the day they did, and SOXX had not gotten the outside boost when it needed one, SOXX would likely have ended the quarter significantly worse than it did.

Who was hot and who was not

The majority of semis lost most of their gains as the quarter came to an end, but not all did so as can be seen by looking at how the individual stocks present in SOXX performed. The 30 stocks represented in SOXX are Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Micron (MU), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Applied Materials (AMAT), Marvell (MRVL), KLA Corp (KLAC), Intel (INTC), Qualcomm (QCOM), Texas Instruments (TXN), Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR), Teradyne (TER), Lam Research (LRCX), Analog Devices (ADI), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), ASML (ASML), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company or TSMC (TSM), Microchip (MCHP), On Semiconductor/Onsemi (ON), Entegris (ENTG), Credo Technology Group (CRDO), Macom Technology Solutions (MTSI), Astera Labs (ALAB), Nova (NVMI), ASE Technology (ASX), Rambus (RMBS), Skyworks (SWKS), STMicroelectronics (STM), United Microelectronics Corporation (UMC) and Arm Holdings (ARM). The table above shows why Q1 2026 was not a bad quarter for the sector overall as most semis ended the quarter with gains, something that can’t be said of other sectors. Of the 30 stocks in SOXX, 21 ended Q1 2026 up and just 9 finished in the red. On the other hand, most ended the quarter well off their highs as the war in Iran resulted in all but four to lose ground in the month of March.

The four that managed to buck the trend in what was a tough month for semis, and stocks in general, were MRVL, ARM, STM and AMD. MRVL’s performance in particular stood out with a gain of 21.25% in March because if not for the stock gaining in March, which would not have happened without help from NVDA, it would have ended the quarter with losses. In contrast, MU was the worst performer in the list above during March with a loss of 18.07% as some of the air that had build up in memory stocks started to escape.

For the entire quarter, TER ended up as the winner with a gain of 53.16% in Q1 2026, ahead of runner-ups ENTG with a gain of 39.16% and ASX with a gain of 34.66%. At the other end of the spectrum were the losers, which included CRDO with a loss of 34.76%, ALAB with a loss of 34.12% and QCOM with a loss of 24.71% during the quarter.

In the past 12 months, only two, QCOM and SWKS are trading below where they did a year ago as the other 28 were able to trade higher. Both also have the distinction of having steadily fallen in the past year and NXPI came close to it. In contrast, MRVL, STM and ARM were able to steadily add to their gains and NVMI just missed out on achieving this feat.

Still, semis have lost a step. Semis usually outperform the indexes like the S&P500 on the way up, but they underperformed during the selloff that hit most stocks in March. The trend was decidedly positive for semis, but it has become less so more recently. The top gainer on a TTM basis is MU with an impressive gain of 288.81%, for instance, but it has progressively slowed down during the past year, culminating in the 18.07% loss in March.

Memory stocks have done exceptionally well, particularly after memory prices for NAND and DRAM in particular spiked higher in the summer of 2025, but the slowdown could portend an ending of what could turn out to be a bubble in retrospect. This included DRAM prices multiplying by 4-5 or even more times in just a few months.

It does not have to carry over into the AI world as a whole, but this could foreshadow problems for AI as a catalyst because the boom in memory prices was a direct consequence of the boom in AI. It was AI that caused demand for memory to spike, boosting prices, but if AI starts to falter, then that will have negative consequences for the semiconductor sector as AI is core to semiconductor demand, especially if it is combined with headwinds elsewhere.

Why war in the Middle East muddies the outlook for semis

Going into 2026 semis had the backing of two powerful tailwinds, easing in Fed policy and the boom in AI. Both promised to tilt the semiconductor sector higher in the new year, but the outbreak of the war in the Middle East has the potential to upend both these factors to the point that the outlook becomes more of a toss-up at the very least, depending on whether one or both serve as a headwind or tailwind for the sector.

The fighting in the Middle East could affect semis in multiple ways. For instance, while AI has boomed, which had provided all sorts of benefits for many semis, it requires a constant infusion of capital to keep the boom going. The Gulf States like Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates and Qatar were expected to become big contributors of fresh capital flowing into AI, but with restrictions imposed on the Strait of Hormuz, their ability to export energy and generate capital is severely diminished. This could hurt those AI companies that were looking at the Gulf states for funding.

One such project is Stargate UAE, which involves such high-profile names in the AI space like OpenAI and NVDA. The Gulf States were expected to become a destination for more datacenters due to relatively low energy prices and other incentives, but the fighting makes this less likely, which in turn could impact semiconductor demand as the chips needed to be housed in these datacenters may no longer be acquired. Note also that some datacenters already in the region have come under attack.

Furthermore, if the fighting in the Middle East continues, the Gulf states will eventually be confronted with big holes in their budgets, which could force these countries to liquidate some of their assets to raise funds. This could involve the selling of equities, which would work its way down to semis. Instead of a tailwind, semis may be confronted with a lasting headwind due to recent events in the Middle East.

There are other ways for semis to be affected. One involves physical damage to local facilities. INTC, for instance, looks especially vulnerable in this regard as it own several facilities in Israel. INTC could potentially suffer damages well into the billions, which would also come at a bad time for INTC and its efforts to power a turnaround from the setbacks suffered in recent years.

Supply chain disruptions could also become an issue for semis, particularly those directly involved in chipmaking, with the region a major source of inputs like helium. However, of all the potential headwinds, the biggest one with the most potential to affect semis is a change in Fed policy. This is because as a consequence of the outbreak of war in the Middle East, the Fed could shift from the accommodation expected at the start of 2026 to something much less so.

At the start of 2026, the Fed was expected to serve as a tailwind for semis since it was on the path to monetary easing, but the recent events in the Middle East have thrown this outlook into doubt. The Federal Reserve had cut the federal funds rate or FFR by three times in 2025 for a reduction of 75 basis points and more rate cuts were expected in 2026.

However, futures have assigned the highest probability to no rate cuts in 2026, which means the FFR could stay at the current 3.50-3.75%. Futures also project there to be just one rate cut of 25 basis points in 2027. This is actually more pessimistic than the Fed’s own outlook for interest rate policy going by the latest dot plots.

In the worst case, the war in the Middle East continues, which keeps oil prices high and which fuels upward pressure on inflation in the economy. Not only would this make the Fed reluctant to stimulate as the market had come to expect, but it is not impossible for the Fed to be forced to hike rates, or the exact opposite of what semis had counted on. Semis tend to not do well when the Fed starts to tighten monetary policy.

What to expect in Q2 2026

The outlook for semis has become less clear heading into Q2 2026. While individual names may be able to buck the trend, semis as a whole have as much a shot at adding to their gains in Q2 2026 as they have at suffering losses. The determining factor here is that it depends on how the situation in the Middle East evolves.

While some recent comments suggest an end to the fighting in the Middle is not far away, the reality on the ground is that more military forces are being send to the region. This includes ground forces and close air support or CAS aircraft like the A-10, or the type of combat aircraft that works in close conjunction with ground troops. It begs the question why there is a need for more forces if the winding down of the fighting is not far away.

At this time, a continuation of the fighting in the Middle East looks more likely for the simple reason that a halt in the fighting at this point is undesirable for both sides. If, for instance, the war stops at this time, Iran would remain in control of the Hormuz Strait, which would give Iran immense geopolitical power as many nations, especially in the region would have to cater to it, something very likely to be unacceptable to the U.S. and its allies. The U.S. needs to further degrade the ability of Iran to project force and that requires more fighting.

Similarly, Iran would not want what effectively amounts to a ceasefire or a temporary freeze in the fighting just so the U.S. can restart the war when it better suits the Trump Administration, like after the midterm elections. It would better suit Iran to continue the war as long as it can since that would increase the likelihood of undesirable consequences for the Trump Administration.

If we assume continued fighting as the premise, then this suggests semis are probably in for more turbulence in the second quarter, especially in the early part of the second quarter, if not further downside. Volatility is very likely with wild swings possible as the U.S. knows it has to do everything it can to lower the fallout for energy prices and equity markets, just as Iran knows it must bide for time as the effect of higher energy prices starts to impact daily life.

Investor takeaway

It is said that the first casualty in a war is the truth. Both sides in any war have an interest in denying their opponent knowledge of what they intend to do since the odds of success goes up if you have the element of surprise on your side and the enemy is not prepared or not expecting what you plan on doing. Conversely, the odds of failure goes up if the enemy knows what is coming.

This makes trying to predict the road ahead based on news headlines fraught with risk. This applies to both the long and the short side of the trade. Long/short pairs could be a better alternative than directional trades as the latter may get whiplashed by warring parties that have an interest in shaping public perceptions of the war in the Middle East that aligns with their objectives.

It is unwise to make decisions based on public statements from warring parties. This is applicable to whether or not to be a buyer or seller of stocks, especially trades for the short term. It is better to attach more weight to what is happening on the ground, which includes whether more forces are mobilized to be send over or if there is an increase or decrease in attacks from Iran. Both would be a better indicator of where the war is going and by extension what semis can expect to happen, in Q2 2026 and beyond.

If semis are to regain the vigor they displayed in the early part of Q1 2026, a resolution to the conflict in the Middle East will be required. It might be different for individual stocks, but semis as a group are unlikely to do well if there is a lingering uncertainty as to what lies ahead for the semiconductor sector, especially in terms of Fed policy or whether the Fed will ease or even tighten.

The ideal outcome for semis would be for the U.S. to find a way to decisively defeat Iran. Such an outcome would brighten the outlook for semis and likely set them free on the way higher. On the other hand, the longer Iran succeeds in denying the U.S. a clear victory, the more likely it becomes that the semiconductor sector, as tracked by ETFs like SOXX, starts to feel the heat as the consequences from the fighting in the Middle East starts to make its way around.

data.title: IHD: This Fund Has A Lot Going For It, But The Price Is Above Average
data.description:
The Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund (IHD) is one of the few closed-end funds that investors can use to get emerging market equity exposure without having to focus on a specific individual country or region. In other words, this is one of the only emerging markets closed-end funds that is not a country-specific fund. This could be an appealing characteristic for investors since it can be difficult to obtain good information about many emerging market nations to enable an investor to perform their due diligence. With this fund, a professional management team that watches emerging markets very closely does the work for you and attempts to appropriately manage the portfolio on your behalf. That may be an appealing characteristic, especially for an individual who would rather do other things than spend considerable amounts of time researching the political and economic situations of multiple foreign countries. In addition to this, the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund boasts a 9.94% yield at the current price, so it may be attractive for investors who are looking for a high level of income. Overall, the proposition that this fund makes seems reasonably appealing, so let us take a closer look at it and see if it would be a good addition to our portfolios today.

About The Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund

The website for the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund states that:

[The Fund] invests primarily in dividend producing equity securities of issuers located in emerging markets.

This describes an equity fund, which is something of a rarity among emerging market funds. Regular readers may recall that we have discussed emerging market bond funds in the past, such as in the following articles:

  • TEI: Long-Term Potential, But May Struggle In The Short-Term
  • EDF: Emerging Market Debt Fund Could Benefit From AI-Driven Commodity Price Surge
  • EDD: A Unique CEF That Provides A High Yield And Foreign Currency Exposure

These articles all discuss emerging market closed-end funds that invest in debt securities, not in equities like IHD. However, they might still be worth reading to gain some additional insight into emerging markets. In particular, many emerging market nations are commodity exporters, and a few of them have economies that are highly dependent on the export of a single or a few commodities. As regular readers are likely aware, I suspect that the world will be entering into a commodity supercycle in the near future, as the development and deployment of technologies such as artificial intelligence will require large amounts of copper, nickel, silver, and various rare earth metals. Certain renewable energy technologies, such as solar panels, also require copper and silver, and batteries use lithium and cobalt. Emerging market nations tend to be among the largest producers of these compounds, and as such, the demand growth that these technologies will stimulate in commodities will result in economic growth in the emerging nations that export them. This could benefit not only the companies that produce those commodities, but every company that operates in those countries. After all, a growing economy should result in individuals becoming wealthier, and as they spend their newfound wealth, the profits of all companies in those nations will grow. This should ultimately be beneficial for the stock prices of those companies, which naturally will benefit the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund.

Perhaps surprisingly, many of the largest positions in the fund will likely be familiar to most readers. Here are the ten largest holdings in the portfolio of the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund as of February 28, 2026:

The reason why I said that it is surprising that the fund's largest positions would be familiar to many readers is that many companies that operate in emerging markets are exclusive to those markets and do not get much name recognition or media attention in the developed world. For example, it is somewhat doubtful that CEZ (CZAVF) will be familiar to a typical investor in the United States despite being the largest electric utility in the Czech Republic. The same is true for many other emerging market companies.

The companies that are listed among the largest positions in IHD's portfolio are an exception, however. The largest position in the fund is Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), which is a fairly popular holding in the portfolios of many American technology investors. After all, Taiwan Semiconductor owns the largest semiconductor foundry in the world, and it is the manufacturer of many of the computer processing chips that are found in most electronic products today. This dominant position in the global technology space has resulted in Taiwan Semiconductor being one of the best-performing stocks over the past ten years. As we can see here, this company has crushed the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the past decade:

Over the past ten years, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing's American Depositary Receipt has increased in price by 1,250.83%. This far surpasses the 218.84% total return that the S&P 500 Index has delivered over the same period. Despite this incredibly strong run-up, Taiwan Semiconductor still trades at an attractive valuation compared to the so-called "Magnificent 7" American technology stocks (MAGS). As of the time of writing, Taiwan Semiconductor trades with a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 23.52. Here is how that compares with the "Magnificent 7" companies:

Company Name

Forward Price/Earnings Ratio

Microsoft (MSFT)

22.08

Apple (AAPL)

30.05

Alphabet (GOOGL)

25.70

Meta Platforms (META)

19.16

NVIDIA (NVDA)

21.19

Amazon.com (AMZN)

27.19

Tesla (TSLA)

183.27

We can see that the price-to-earnings range for the "Magnificent 7" technology stocks is 19.16 to 30.05, with Tesla's 183.27 ratio as an outlier. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing is currently trading with a valuation that is in this range, so the company is not overvalued (at least not any more than any large-capitalization technology stock) despite the huge run-up in the price that has occurred over the past ten years.

As we can see in the fund's largest positions list, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing accounts for 13.37% of the fund's total assets. As such, the fund has a rather outsized position to this individual stock, and investors in this fund should be confident that it will perform well prior to buying shares of the fund. After all, given the magnitude of the fund's exposure to this company, weak performance from TSM will end up having an adverse impact on the fund itself.

Another name among the fund's largest positions that may be familiar to many readers is SK hynix. This company does not have an American ticker symbol, but trades on the KRX Exchange in South Korea under the ticker symbol 000660. Much like Taiwan Semiconductor, SK hynix is a manufacturer of specialized components used in many pieces of modern-day technology. In this case, the components are dynamic random-access memory chips and flash memory chips. These two components are essential for just about every technological gadget used by both individuals and businesses. For example, all computers, smartphones, and tablets use random-access memory. Most computers, all smartphones, and tablets use flash memory. While not all of the ones that you may think of use parts specifically made by SK hynix, the fact remains that just about every piece of technology uses components such as those made by this company.

The fact that SK hynix is primarily a manufacturer of both volatile and non-volatile memory chips is the reason why it may be familiar to many investors, as this position has resulted in it being in the financial news quite a few times in recent memory. As we all know, many of the major American technology companies have been investing heavily in constructing data centers and buying servers. This is an attempt to support the widespread deployment of artificial intelligence. Unfortunately, the orders that have been placed by these companies have been so large that they have caused a shortage of dynamic random-access memory. From Geeky-Gadgets:

DRAM shortage isn't just a supply chain hiccup; it's a full-blown crisis reshaping industries from AI development to consumer electronics. Prices for memory products have skyrocketed by as much as 300%, leaving businesses and individuals scrambling to adapt.

This is similar to the situation that occurred during the latter half of the previous decade when cryptocurrency miners were buying up enormous numbers of graphical processing units and caused a shortage in that market. In this case, it is the hyperscalers who are aggressively purchasing more memory than manufacturers can produce and not really caring too much about the cost of purchasing memory. This has caused the stock price of SK hynix to surge over the past year:

On April 1, 2025, SK hynix was trading at roughly 197,000 South Korean won. The closing price on April 2, 2026, was 830,000 South Korean won. That is a 321.32% gain in a year. This could be one reason why the company accounts for 3.52% of IHD's portfolio, as the share price increase over the past year may have caused its proportional representation in the portfolio to increase. After all, this is a better performance than many other emerging market stocks have been able to deliver over the same period. Thus, if all else is equal, the proportion of the fund's total assets that SK hynix represents would have increased over the period.

There are a few other companies among the largest positions of the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund that may be familiar to many readers. For example, Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), Alibaba Group (BABA), Tencent (TCEHY), and ICICI Bank (IBN) might all be relatively familiar names. This may be comforting to some investors who might otherwise be nervous about investing in an emerging markets fund. After all, as was previously mentioned, many of the companies that operate in emerging markets are ones that do not exist in the developed world. As such, an investor in a typical emerging market equity fund might be investing in companies that they have never heard of and do not know anything about. That is not the case with the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund. As the largest positions in this fund are mostly familiar names, it is easier to do research on them, and it may be easier to get comfortable with owning them.

The website for the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund states that the fund primarily invests in dividend-paying common equities. Dividend investing is perhaps not as popular in the United States as it once was. I can remember back around the time of the financial crisis that there were numerous highly popular blogs spread around the Internet that focused on dividend-paying common stocks and strategies built on owning common stocks with a long history of annual dividend increases. Nowadays, other than a few services available through Seeking Alpha's marketplace (including mine), these strategies are much less commonly discussed. I suspect that one reason for this is that the ultra-low-interest-rate policy that prevailed throughout most of the developed world following the 2009 recession prompted investors to focus on long-duration strategies that favored capital gains over income. As many of us can likely recall, for nearly all of the decade following the financial crisis, the best-performing stocks in the American market were the mega-capitalization technology stocks, and most of those companies did not pay dividends at that time. Dividend stocks, in general, underperformed. This did not stop with the surge in interest rates that followed the pandemic (although non-yielding technology stocks were hit worse than many other sectors). As Morningstar points out, dividend strategies underperformed in 2024 as well, due mostly to the strong performance of technology stocks that have either no yield or a negligible one. The technology sector (XLK) also outperformed the S&P 500 Index (SP500) in 2025:

Thus, the fact that the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund invests primarily in dividend-paying common stocks may not earn it attention from investors seeking to maximize their performance. However, foreign stocks are somewhat different from American stocks when it comes to dividends and relative performance. As Morningstar states:

The Morningstar Global Markets ex-US High Dividend Yield Index and the Morningstar Global ex-US Dividend Growth Index have both enjoyed strong runs. The higher-yielding half of the dividend-paying universe for developed- and emerging-markets equities outside the US, as well as the shares of companies consistently growing their cash payouts, are comfortably ahead of the broad market over the past five years.

In other words, dividend-paying foreign stocks tend to do fairly well relative to their non-yielding peers. One reason for this is that foreign investors tend to have a stronger preference for receiving dividends than American investors. In 2024, researchers at the National University of Singapore, the University of Macau, and Huazhong University of Science and Technology conducted a study that found that historically dividend-paying stocks outperform non-dividend-paying stocks globally by an average of 0.58% per month. Annualized, that is a 6.96% return advantage that the dividend-paying stocks enjoy. We can certainly speculate on the reasons for this, and personally, I suspect that taxation might play a role. Regardless of the actual reason, though, the fund's focus on dividend-paying stocks may actually be the correct strategy for maximizing returns from an emerging markets portfolio.

As might be expected, then, many of the largest positions in IHD's portfolio pay a dividend. Here are their yields:

Company Name

Current Yield

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing

1.04%

Samsung Electronics Co.

0.47%

HON HAI Precision Industry (HNHAF)

2.28%

Itau Unibanco Holding (ITUB)

While all of these companies pay dividends, most of them have yields that are not especially attractive. For example, we can see that the MSCI Emerging Markets ETF, which tracks the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (an equity index), has a yield of 2.12%. There are only two stocks shown that have a higher yield than the index, which clearly tells us that nearly all of these stocks have below-average yields. With that said, though, several of these stocks only have relatively low yields because of the fact that they have experienced substantial capital appreciation over the past few months. SK hynix is an example of this, as obviously its yield would be much more attractive if its stock price had not gotten run up over the past twelve months. The same is true for Delta Electronics, which has risen by 287.01% over the past twelve months:

There is an inverse correlation between yield and share price. Whenever a company's share price increases more rapidly than its dividend, the yield goes down. That is what occurred here, and it has resulted in Delta Electronics having a lower yield than it would have at the year-ago price.

As is the case with SK hynix, Delta Electronics' share price has surged due to hyperscaler demand for its products for use in data centers and servers. Delta Electronics manufactures computer fans and rectifiers, among other products that are used in data centers.

Another thing that is worth noting is that the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund is one of the only closed-end funds of any type that counts multiple stocks among its top ten holdings to have more than doubled in price over the past year. This could certainly provide investors with some confidence regarding the fund managers' ability to pick strong-performing stocks, but it is also possible that management just got lucky. In either case, it is nice to see, but it also opens investors up to a few risks. Perhaps the most important, and the most concerning, risk that we should keep in mind is that the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund has a great deal of exposure to the information technology sector. As shown here, 34.38% of the fund's total assets were invested in that particular sector on February 28, 2026:

The fund's exposure to technology is greater than that of any other sector, and it does weaken our case for investing in emerging markets as a proxy for commodities. The fund does, however, have much higher exposure to the Basic Materials sector than does the S&P 500 Index or most funds that invest in American indices, so there is still some commodities exposure there. The risk here, though, is that the high exposure to technology and the high exposure to those companies making items that are used in data centers specifically is that if hyperscalers cut back on their capital expenditures or if artificial intelligence ends up not being as profitable as analysts currently expect, then companies such as SK hynix and Delta Electronics could give up the gains that they delivered in the past year. Naturally, if that happens, then any investor buying the fund's shares today will suffer those losses.

The Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund Versus Index ETFs

At the current share price, the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund boasts a 9.94% yield. This is a substantially higher yield than the yield provided by any major broad-market domestic, global, or international common equity index. We can see that in this chart:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index (SPY)

1.13%

NASDAQ 100 Index (QQQ)

0.48%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

1.02%

MSCI World Index (URTH)

1.52%

MSCI All-Countries World Index (ACWI)

1.57%

MSCI All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index (ACWX)

2.73%

MSCI Emerging Markets Index

2.12%

The highest-yielding of these indices is the MSCI All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index, which is also one of only two broad-market global equity indices that specifically excludes any American stocks. We can also see that only two of the equity indices have yields above 2%, and these are also the only two that do not include American stocks. This supports the statements that I made earlier in this article about foreign investors generally having a higher preference for dividends and about foreign stocks frequently having higher yields than American ones. However, even these indices have a far lower yield than the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund.

Unfortunately, the price performance of IHD has been considerably worse over the past ten years than that of the two international indices. As shown here, the fund's share price over the past ten years is down 8.55%:

We can see that, in direct contrast to the two indices, the fund's share price was largely flat over the period. This is far worse than the two indices, each of which delivered price appreciation of more than 70%. This may be something of a turn-off for most investors. After all, nobody wants to lose money on any sort of investment. This is particularly true for investors who have a focus on income, as many such investors are retirees who may not have the easiest time replacing their principal. As such, this performance is certainly going to be discouraging.

Furthermore, in previous articles, I have pointed out the need to ensure that an investor's principal manages to retain its purchasing power over the long-term. This is necessary in order to ensure that your lifestyle does not deteriorate over the course of an extended retirement. According to the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers, it was necessary to grow your principal by 36.58% over the April 2016 to February 2026 period in order to ensure that the purchasing power remained the same. Both of the broad-market international indices shown above managed to accomplish this, but the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund did not.

However, one of the defining characteristics of closed-end funds, such as the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Fund, is that they deliver the bulk of their returns via the distributions that they pay out to their shareholders. In fact, there are some high-performing funds that basically have relatively stable share prices over the long term because they pay out all of their capital gains and net investment income to their investors. Exchange-traded index funds do not do this. Rather, index funds rarely realize capital gains and simply pay out their net investment income while relying on share price appreciation to deliver returns to shareholders. As such, we should look at total return rather than price return when comparing IHD to either index funds or other closed-end funds in order to get an apples-to-apples comparison. When we do that, we get the following chart for the trailing ten-year period:

As shown, the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund delivered a 162.85% total return over the past ten years, actually beating both the ACWX and the EEM index funds. Thus, the performance of this fund was certainly not nearly as bad as would be suggested by our previous simple look at the price performance alone. In fact, it is one of the rare closed-end funds that actually managed to beat the most comparable indices over an extended period of time. Earlier in this article, I mentioned that the fund's management has done a fairly good job at picking stocks in recent times, and it appears that this could extend over longer periods of time. However, we have to note that investors would have only realized the above performance had they reinvested all of the distributions that IHD paid out over the past decade. Many investors who actually have the generation of income as a fairly high priority might not want to do this and would rather take the distributions as income to spend. While this is certainly fine to do (as your goals and your money are yours to do with as you please), it probably would have been a good idea to reinvest at least some of the distributions in order to ensure that the nominal dollar amount of your position in the fund kept up with inflation. After all, this fund was unable to achieve that through share price appreciation alone.

Another thing that is worth mentioning is that the composition of the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund is somewhat different from that of the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF. This chart shows the sector allocation of the exchange-traded index fund:

If we refer back to the previous chart, we can immediately see that the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund is overweight to both information technology and industrials compared to the index. IHD is also underweight in consumer discretionary and energy relative to the index. The underweight position to energy was probably not the best allocation to be holding in March, as energy actually performed quite well relative to many other sectors. After all, the Iran War and Iranian retaliation have resulted in a spike in crude oil prices. This tends to be good for energy stocks, and indeed the iShares Global Energy ETF (IXC) has substantially outperformed the MSCI All-Countries World Index over the past month:

An underweight position to energy would have resulted in the fund's returns being worse over the past month than they would have been if it had a higher allocation to the energy sector. Admittedly, though, the military strikes on Iran were something of a surprise, and it would have been impossible for the fund's management to predict the attacks in advance. Prior to the outbreak of hostilities, having a low allocation to energy relative to other sectors looked like a pretty good position to hold.

The Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund Versus Peer CEFs

There are relatively few broad market emerging markets equity funds in the market, which may be due to the fact that emerging markets as a group underperformed the U.S. markets during the decade between the financial crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic. Investors do not generally like underperformance, so the underperformance of emerging markets likely reduced interest among investors in having exposure to this asset class. Most fund managers do not want to start up a fund if not very many people want to invest in it.

With that said, though, there are three emerging market closed-end funds in the market that do not focus on any specific country or region:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund

Equity-Emerging Market Equity

9.94%

Templeton Emerging Markets Fund (EMF)
