abrdn Emerging Markets ex-China Fund (AEF)

Equity-Emerging Market Equity

11.20%

These three are the only three emerging market closed-end equity funds in the market that give the fund's management the flexibility to invest in any emerging market nation. There are, however, three more funds available that have portfolios limited to a single emerging market:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Mexico Equity and Income Fund (MXE)

Equity-Emerging Market Equity

1.77%

The Mexico Fund (MXF)

Equity-Emerging Market Equity

6.59%

The Central and Eastern Europe Fund (CEE)

Equity-Emerging Market Equity

2.13%

Unlike the three funds shown in the first chart in this section, these three funds impose limits on where the fund manager is allowed to deploy capital. The fund's management does not have the ability to move money to locations that have stronger fundamentals or growth potential. As such, investors themselves have to make the decision on whether or not they wish to have exposure to a given country or region of the world. That, naturally, involves a larger time commitment, as the investor has to do research on a given part of the world in order to make an informed decision about whether or not to invest in that nation.

Finally, there are a few single-country closed-end funds that, as the name suggests, invest their assets in equity securities from a single nation in the world. A fund such as The Korea Fund (KF) or the Morgan Stanley India Investment Fund (IIF) falls into this category. As with the regional funds, an investor in one of these single-country funds needs to do the research on each individual nation in order to determine whether or not investing there is appropriate for them. It is a much larger time commitment, and many retirees may not want to become full-time portfolio managers. As such, the flexibility of a fund such as the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund might be appealing as it provides a one-stop solution, so to speak.

Out of the funds with maximum flexibility, the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund is the median fund in terms of yield. We can see clearly that AEF has a higher yield than IHD, and EMF has a much lower one. The compositions of these funds are quite different, too. For example, the Templeton Emerging Markets Fund has 27.60% of its assets invested in semiconductor manufacturing companies, 1.87% invested in software, and another 11.96% invested in technology hardware and equipment. That puts EMF at 41.43% exposure to technology, which is higher than even IHD's overweight position in the same sector. That could, at least partially, explain why EMF has a lower yield than IHD, as the technology sector tends to be one of the lowest-yielding sectors, regardless of the country that we are investing in. The abrdn Emerging Markets ex-China Fund does not provide a breakdown of its holdings by sector, but the ten largest positions in its portfolio are very different from those in IHD's portfolio. Here are the largest positions in AEF's portfolio:

Once again, we see Taiwan Semiconductor, Samsung Electronics, SK hynix, and Delta Electronics. However, the rest of the companies are very different from the portfolio of IHD. As such, we can expect the performance of this fund to be quite different from the performance of the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund.

The share price performance of the three multi-country emerging markets funds over the past ten years has certainly been very different:

As shown, IHD was the only one of the three funds to actually experience a share price decline over the past ten years. Both of the other two funds delivered strong share price appreciation, but EMF was the only one to retain its purchasing power in real terms. As mentioned earlier, the CPI-U rose by 36.58% over the period, so it was necessary to do some reinvestment of distributions with either IHD or AEF in order to keep the nominal size of a position flat in real terms.

As I mentioned earlier, total return tends to be a better way to compare the relative performance of closed-end funds than price return. After all, the yields of these three funds are all over the place, and the distributions are investment profits that shareholders receive in addition to any price movement. As such, we should consider them when evaluating funds. If we do that, we get the following chart for all three funds over the past ten years:

We can see that IHD slightly edged out AEF in terms of total return. This is perhaps surprising given that AEF has delivered better share price performance and currently has a higher yield than IHD. However, IHD has boasted considerably higher distributions in the past than it has today, so that makes a difference. The Templeton Emerging Markets Fund has, however, done better than both of them. While that could suggest that investors may want to favor EMF over the other two funds, we should keep two things in mind:

  1. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.
  2. The Templeton Emerging Markets Fund has a lower yield than either of the other two funds, so anyone who chooses it is accepting a lower level of current income.

Some investors who want to maximize their incomes are willing to accept a lower level of overall performance in order to get that higher yield. IHD does have a substantially higher yield, so I leave it up to each individual to make up their own mind in that respect.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund was on July 18, 2024. As such, approximately one year and eight months have passed since our last discussion. There have been a number of events that have occurred over the period, including the establishment of a more aggressive tariff regime in the United States and a surge in technology company capital expenditures to finance the construction of data centers to support AI technology. Emerging markets in general tend to be manufacturing economies (at least compared to developed markets), and as such, they would be impacted by both of these trends. After all, many of the physical items used in data centers and in servers are manufactured in emerging markets. We discussed this earlier in this article.

The price of the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund is up 22.19% since the last time that we discussed this fund. That is a worse performance than either the ACWX or the EEM indices delivered:

However, in terms of total return, the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund outperformed both indices by quite a lot over the period:

As we can see, investors in the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund have realized a 49.31% total return since the last time that we discussed this fund. We can also see that both emerging markets and international stocks did well over the period, as both asset classes delivered strong performance after April of 2025. This was at least partially due to the weakening U.S. dollar over the period, as a weakening U.S. dollar usually causes the share prices of foreign stocks to rise in U.S. dollar terms. However, there were other factors that drove strength in emerging markets over the period, including rising commodity prices and a surge in demand for technological products. In either case, we can clearly see that investors who purchased shares of IHD at the time that my last article was published have managed to do quite well.

Distribution Analysis

The website for the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of total return. This makes sense for an equity fund, but as is the case with most closed-end funds, IHD primarily delivers its total return through direct payments made to its shareholders. The fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.0550 per share ($0.66 per share annually), which gives it a 9.94% yield at the current share price. This is the same distribution level that this fund was paying the last time we looked at it, but it has boasted higher distributions in the past. For example, back in 2022, this fund was paying $0.18 per share quarterly. The current distribution is only $0.1650 per share quarterly, so the distribution today is lower than what the fund had in 2022 or earlier. That is disappointing, but of course, anyone who purchases the fund's shares today will not be negatively affected by the fact that it has cut its distribution in the past. The important thing for our purchases right now is how well the fund can carry its current distribution going forward.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund is the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on August 31, 2025. This report is several months old at present, which is unfortunate. However, the fund does not typically release its annual reports until mid-May, so it is the newest financial report that we will likely have for this fund for at least another month or so. Additionally, this report is newer than the one that we had available to us the last time that we discussed this fund, so it should work reasonably well to provide us with an update.

For the six-month period that ended on August 31, 2025, the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund reported a net investment income of $1,455,306. This was nowhere near enough to cover the $5,856,604 that the fund paid out in distributions over the period. This is not necessarily a problem, though, as most equity funds cannot cover their distributions solely through net investment income. This is due to the simple fact that common equities in general tend to have very low yields.

The fund did manage to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on August 31, 2025, the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund reported net realized gains of $3,678,542 along with $10,797,647 in net unrealized gains. When combined with net investment income, the fund had total investment profits of $15,931,495:

This was more than enough to cover $5,856,604 in distribution payments with money left over. However, we should note that the fund did have to rely on its unrealized capital gains to fully cover the distribution. The total of the net investment income and the net realized gains was $5,133,848 over the period. This was not quite enough to cover the distribution. While this can be concerning, we probably do not have to worry too much. The fund only had to use $722,756 of its net unrealized gains to achieve full distribution coverage. That is such a small proportion of the fund's net unrealized gains that it is highly unlikely that they will be erased by a market correction before they can be realized. Thus, everything should be okay here.

Valuation

Shares of the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund are currently trading at a 4.19% discount on net asset value. This is a smaller discount than the 7.20% discount that the fund's shares have had on average over the past month.

The fund's current price is also somewhat expensive given its historical price range. Over the past five years, shares of the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund have traded at prices ranging from a 1.82% discount to a 17.22% discount on net asset value. The average price over the five-year period was a 10.83% discount on net asset value, which is a better price than what the fund has today. This could suggest that a better price may become available in the future for investors who are willing to wait.

Here is how the current price of the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund compares with that of its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund

(4.19%)

Templeton Emerging Markets Fund

(11.79%)

abrdn Emerging Markets ex-China Fund

(11.40%)

The Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund clearly looks somewhat expensive compared to its peers despite the fact that all three of these funds are trading at prices that are lower than the intrinsic value of their assets. When we combine this with the fact that the fund's five-year average price is better than what it has today, we can conclude that it might be advantageous to wait for a larger discount before buying in.

Expenses

The semi-annual report states that IHD had an annualized expense ratio of 1.48% for the six-month period that ended on August 31, 2025.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund is an emerging markets equity fund that primarily invests in dividend-paying common stocks and has historically beaten the emerging market indices. The fund has high exposure to a few of the companies that are benefiting from the artificial intelligence boom by supplying the products that hyperscalers need for servers and data centers. In addition, emerging markets in general tend to benefit from rising commodity prices, and that is also likely to be a trend over the remainder of this year. In short, the Voya Emerging Markets High Dividend Equity Fund appears to have a lot going for it, but its current price is somewhat above average.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nano Dimension: Proving The Markforged Acquisition Is More Than Just Revenue Growth
data.description:
Nano Dimension (NNDM) is a leading company in the 3D printing additive manufacturing industry I have been following.

NNDM has two revenue streams, Products, where majority of its revenue is generated from, and Services. Products revenue comes from the sale of 3D printing machines and associated materials, while Services revenue comes from technical support and maintenance. In my view, the 3D printing and additive manufacturing market remains competitive and evolving due to the innovative nature of the solutions themselves. NNDM’s competitors include sizable players like 3D Systems (DDD) or Stratasys.

Since going public, share performance has been largely disappointing. At one point after going public, NNDM was trading above $50 per share. However, share price has been on gradual decline since then. Most recently trading at $1.67 per share, the stock is currently trading near its lowest point, down 80% in the past five years. Nonetheless, the fact that it has been up around 7% YTD and around 8% on a 1-year basis suggests that it has gained some momentum lately.

I initiate my coverage with a buy rating. My 1-year price target of $2.03 presents around 22% upside from the current level.

Financial Reviews

Fundamentals remain mixed, in my opinion. Revenue growth was mainly driven by the Markforged acquisition, while profitability improved slightly despite still being in the red for the most part. In Q4 2025, revenue growth improved significantly by 142% YoY to $35.3 million, much due to the revenue contribution from Markforged. However, in the absence of that, NNDM basically saw a flat YoY growth, as commented on by the management:

Revenue for the fourth quarter was $35.3 million, representing a year-over-year growth of approximately 142% compared to $14.6 million in the fourth quarter of 2024. This increase was driven primarily by the inclusion of Markforged, which contributed $20.7 million. Excluding Markforged, Nano Dimension's stand-alone revenue was approximately $14.6 million in line with the prior year as underlying growth offset the impact of divestments.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Overall, FY revenue was $102.4 million, which is clearly better for the company, as it should give it a better base and scale to offset its fixed costs going forward to improve its profitability.

Profitability saw a slight improvement, with adjusted EBITDA (aEBITDA) loss narrowing to -$53.2 million for FY 2025 from around -$63 million last year. Meanwhile, GAAP gross margin saw a contraction to 33% in FY 2025 from around 40% the prior year, potentially due to the impact of non-cash step-up inventory amortization due to the Markforged acquisition. Upon adjustment, the non-GAAP version of gross margin actually saw a YoY improvement to 46.9%, as described by management:

Gross profit for the year was $48.1 million with an adjusted gross margin of approximately 46.9% compared to $26.2 million and 45.4% in the prior year. This growth was primarily driven by the inclusion of Markforged.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Liquidity remains decent, with NNDM ending the year with $459.6 million in cash, deposits, equivalents, and the like. This number is down from the over $500 million last year, which is normal due to the acquisitions and buybacks.

Meanwhile, the cash flow picture does not seem compelling, in my view. NNDM has mostly been burning through operating cash flow (OCF). In FY 2025, OCF loss almost quadrupled to -$70 million, suggesting that NNDM is today not a self-sustaining business. At the same time, NNDM has also made share repurchases, possibly in an effort to maintain its share prices, which I believe has produced minimal value so far.

Catalyst

Overall, I think that catalysts remain minimal to moderate. Given NNDM’s negative cash flow and profitability situations, I believe the management’s initiative to continue cost discipline to lower burn rate as well as realize the full potential of operational synergies from the Markforged acquisition should present a key lever for re-rating, above anything else.

The truth of the matter is that with the share price seeing a decent 7% upside over the past year, at least we could have concluded that the market has viewed the Markforged acquisition positively. In my opinion, it indeed so far has produced positive results for NNDM. First of all, Markforged’s industrial composite and NNDM’s electronics capabilities combined should unlock not only diversification of capabilities but also client base. But more importantly, the key thing is the prospect of synergies to create a scale that unlocks margin expansion through recurring revenue.

For NNDM, specifically, it becomes very important since reaching operating profitability will be sort of a proof that the 3D additive manufacturing industry is attractive enough for investors. In the earnings call, the management has also provided some guidance into FY 2026 that I think could drive some market confidence if NNDM executes it well:

As such, we are implementing annual guidance for 2026. For 2026, we expect revenue in the range of $130 million to $140 million, representing over 30% growth at the midpoint compared to 2025, which included a partial year contribution from Markforged following its acquisition in the second quarter of 2025.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

With that kind of performance, the management also expects to see aEBITDA loss narrowing to -$40 million to -$50 million. Overall, this suggests a continual improvement and stronger picture for NNDM that tells the market it is on the right track for a breakeven narrative. Nonetheless, while it may result in some upward trends into the year, I would not discount any potential for unsustainable share appreciation. This is why the strength of the catalysts would largely be minimal or moderate, in my view.

Risk

I believe risk remains moderate to high. First of all, as I mentioned earlier, I think that NNDM’s FY 2026 re-rating prospect heavily relies on the successful integration of Markforged to realize the financial benefits from the business combinations. However, I believe that integration is generally challenging, and given NNDM’s loss-making situation, falling a bit short of the revenue target could result in share price volatility.

Another risk factor I foresee is the geopolitical instability involving Iran and Israel today, where NNDM is based. In 2023, for instance, NNDM’s Israel facility was damaged due to the missile strike following the October 7 war:

In 2023, the Group’s warehouse located in the south of Israel suffered physical damage due to a direct missile hit related to the Iron Swords War. As a result, damaged inventory in the amount of $5.0 million was written off. The damage was covered by government authorities and the Company’s insurance policy, part of which was received in 2023, and the remainder in 2024. The net excess over the cost of the inventory damaged, was recognized as other income, $0.4 million in 2024 and $3.8 million in 2023.

Source: 10K.

As such, if the ongoing tension today results in similar disruptions, I believe market confidence could drop, even if any potential damage is covered by the government and insurance policies. With the stock currently trading around $1 , managing these risks even becomes more important for NNDM to avoid a delisting trigger.

Valuation / Pricing

My target price for NNDM is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs. bear scenarios:

  1. Bull scenario (50% probability) assumptions - I project successful integration with Markforged, enabling NNDM to deliver revenue at the high end of its guidance, with aEBITDA loss within the expected range as well. I expect NNDM to break $2 per share, with 4.5x P/S, as NNDM slowly recovers from the cash-burning reputation and demonstrates operational scale.

  2. Bear scenario (50% probability) assumptions - I project NNDM to see some issues in the integration, with integration costs turning out higher than expected, making NNDM miss not only the revenue guidance but also its aEBITDA target. P/S to contract to 2x as delisting fear looms, with the stock trading closer to $1.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $2 per share, presenting a potential upside of around 22% from the current level. At this point, I would give the stock a buy rating.

Conclusion

NNDM continues to strengthen its position as a leader in the 3D printing industry, especially post-Markforged acquisition. While the recent acquisition has proven to be financially beneficial for NNDM so far, with revenue expected to hit $130 million in FY 2026 due to the business combination, my view is that any significant re-rating will only be driven by NNDM’s ability to prove that it could reach breakeven through the operating scale it has unlocked. The bull thesis here is that NNDM’s decent progress towards breakeven in 2026 should help it break the $2 per share. My price target analysis has also baked in the potential failure of realizing the synergies, and after all that, it seems that at the current level, NNDM presents a decent opportunistic buy opportunity.

data.title: Microsoft And Its Real Value
data.description:
Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) has been perceived as one of the strongest in the tech world. The stock, however, has experienced real pressure in recent times and has been on a sharp decline. This pullback has been concerning to investors, especially as the company has begun to drag behind some of its mega-cap rivals in terms of market performance. Nonetheless, the business itself has not slowed down. The growth in revenues is still good, and the company is ambitiously working on artificial intelligence, which would carve its future in a big way. The question is, therefore, will this be a mere rough patch or an actual opportunity?

We cannot overlook the decline, considering the recent performance. The shares are also trading at a loss of around 24% year-to-date, making it one of their weakest starts in history. Due to this fact, its valuation levels have dropped significantly, and its forward P/E ratio is now trading at levels that are lower than the industry average. Nonetheless, the fundamental business is performing well. The annual revenue increased at a rate of 17% in the recent quarter, and the forecast growth is about 16% in the future. This discrepancy between the falling stock price and the stable business performance has made many analysts think that the stock may be undervalued and it stands a chance of recovering should the growth proceed as projected.

Artificial intelligence is a big part of its growth story. In the last two years, Microsoft has quietly established a huge presence in AI, mostly based on its cloud platform. Its cloud segment is currently producing more than $50 billion in quarterly revenues, and it is growing at a good pace per year. Azure especially will be able to ride on the increasing demand due to AI and enterprise usage. It is evident that management is still in the initial phases of AI implementation, and there is still much room to grow in the various layers of technology. That places Microsoft in a good position, not only as a software company, but as one of the key stakeholders in the overall digital transformation that is underway.

Meanwhile, the company is also extending its reach by developing a full-scale system of AI. It is trying to create value on every level, starting with cloud infrastructure, through productivity tools and developer platforms. Some products such as Copilot are on the rise; a large backlog will bring in more visibility to the revenue in the future. However, there are some negative sides to this strategy. There is a certain degree of risk in heavy capital expenditure, strain on margins, and the dependency on mega-partners. The high costs of data facilities and computing capability may place a burden on short-term profitability. Nevertheless, such actions indicate a well-intended purpose: Microsoft is not merely getting in on the wave of AI; it is attempting to control as much as it can of the stack.

Snapshot of the Company

MSFT has an impressive company rating score of 75.125 out of 100. In summary, MSFT seems to have above average fundamentals since all, but one category produces good scores.

Before jumping to conclusions, we’ll have to look closer into individual categories to see what’s going on.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer)

Fundamentals

In the chart below, we can see that price per share has been mostly consistent at increasing over the last 10 years, with only two years when share price declined. In 2022, increased interest rates likely caused increased pressure that caused a decline in the share price. In 2026, the stock is again declining, and that is, as we discussed, due to high CapEx, which affects short-term profits. Overall, share price average has grown by about 558% over the past 10 years, or a Compound Annual Growth Rate of 23.28%. This is a fruitful return.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Price Per Share History)

Earnings

Looking closer at the EPS history of Microsoft, we can see that there has been consistency in growth over the years. There have been slight declines in 2018 and 2023, but the trend has been generally increasing, and this implies that the company has been able to expand its profitability despite the varying market conditions.

One of the key factors that contributed to such an increase is the fact that Microsoft stopped depending primarily on traditional software and became more diversified. Its cloud operating business, particularly its Azure service and subscription offerings such as Microsoft 365 business, has generated a robust recurrent flow of revenue. On top of that, the company currently invests much in artificial intelligence that is already contributing to its revenues and may become a more significant driver in the future.

The earnings trend in the case of Microsoft seems to be comparatively predictable and stable. With that being said, the future development will probably be conditional upon the extent to which the company is able to address rising expenses and maintain its expansion of cloud and AI sectors.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – EPS History)

Return on Equity

The return on equity has consistently decreased in the period from 2022 to 2025. While ROE has decreased by nearly 29% in the span of four years, average ROE is still far above expectations at around 39%. For return on equity (ROE), I look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. So, MSFT easily meets my requirements. However, the company’s ROE should be closely watched to see if this downward decline continues.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – ROE History)

Let’s compare the ROE of this company to its industry. The average ROE of 309 Software (Systems and Application) companies is 29.62%.

Therefore, MSFT’s 5-year average of 39% is above average, and so is the current ROE of 33.28.

Return on Invested Capital

The return on invested capital has also been a consistent decliner from 2022 to 2025. ROIC fell nearly 17% during the period, but the 5-year average ROIC is above expectations at around 30%. For return on invested capital (ROIC), I also look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. So, MSFT passes this test as well, and the future trend should continue to be watched.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Return on Invested Capital History)

Gross Margin Percent

The gross margin percent (GMP) has experienced very little fluctuation over the past few years. GMP fell after 2024, but only by 1%. Even with the decline, overall gross margin percent is at very high levels. Five-year average GMP is good at around 68.9%. I typically look for companies with a gross margin percent consistently above 30%. So, MSFT has proven that it can maintain acceptable margins over a long period.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Gross Margin Percent History)

Financial Stability

Looking at other fundamentals involving the balance sheet, we can see that the debt-to-equity is less than 1. This is good news, telling us that the company owns more than it owes.

MSFT’s Current Ratio of 1.39 is satisfactory, indicating it could use assets to pay its short-term debt. Ideally, we’d want to see a Current Ratio of more than 1, so MSFT exceeds this amount.

According to the balance sheet, the company appears to be in good financial health. In the long term, the company has more than enough assets to cover its debts. In the short term, the company is generating enough cash flow to fulfill its obligations.

MSFT currently pays a dividend of 0.95% to its investors and has increased its dividend consistently for 21 years.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Misc. Fundamentals)

Value Vs. Price

The company’s Price-Earnings Ratio of 25 indicates that MSFT might be selling at a low price when comparing MSFT’s current PE Ratio to MSFT’s historical PE Ratio.

The 10-year and 5-year average PE ratios of MSFT have typically been 32.6 and 32.02, respectively.

(Source: BTMA Stock Analyzer – Stock Value)

The Estimated Value of the Stock is $450.12, versus the current stock price of $373. This indicates that MSFT is currently selling at a bargain price.

This analysis shows an average valuation of around $444 per share versus its current price of about $373, this would indicate that Microsoft is undervalued.

However, if we focus only on Analysts’ Future Growth Forecasts, the valuation is $293 - $317 versus a current share price of $373, which indicates that Microsoft is overpriced and the share price may have room to decline more.

Summarizing the Fundamentals

According to the facts, Microsoft is financially stable in the long run as well as in the short term. It has a healthy balance sheet and debt levels that appear sustainable. Its sufficient cash flow and assets can adequately cover the company’s liabilities.

The company has, over the years, recorded very high earnings, and the trend is mostly positive. Much of that strength stems out of how well it has repositioned and diversified into cloud services and, more recently, into artificial intelligence. The overall profitability ratios, such as return on equity, return on invested capital, and gross margins, remain good. With that said, some of these numbers have slightly declined in recent periods, which is probably associated with an increase in costs and excessive expenditure on AI and infrastructure expansion.

It is obvious that Microsoft has a long-term advantage because of the current shift towards cloud computing and AI-powered products. Simultaneously, it does exert a certain strain on short-term margins due to the magnitude of investment involved. Valuation mostly shows that the company is undervalued, with a potential for more share price declines in the future.

Generally, the core elements of Microsoft are quite healthy, although investors should closely monitor the impact of increased costs and building capital expenditures in the coming years.

MSFT Vs. The S&P 500

From the chart below, we can see that MSFT has crushed the general market. MSFT is clearly a high growth stock that continues to be in a growing industry (software). MSFT has achieved explosive growth since 2018. The most recent price dip could indicate a buying opportunity.

Forward-Looking Conclusion

Over the next five years, the analysts that follow this company are expecting it to grow earnings at an average annual rate of 13.76%.

In addition, the average one-year price target for this stock is at $589.9, which is about a 65% increase in a year.

The Expected Annual Compounding Rate of Return is 13.94%.

Does MSFT Pass My Checklist?

  1. Company Rating 70+ out of 100? YES (75)
  2. Share Price Compound Annual Growth Rate > 12%? YES (23%)
  3. Earnings history mostly increasing? YES
  4. ROE (5-year average 16% or greater)? YES (39%)
  5. ROIC (5-year average 16% or greater)? YES (30%)
  6. Gross Margin % (5-year average > 30%)? YES (69%)
  7. Debt-to-Equity (less than 1)? YES
  8. Current Ratio (greater than 1)? YES
  9. Outperformed S&P 500 during most of the past 10 years? YES
  10. Do I think this company will continue to successfully sell their same main product/service for the next 10 years? YES

MSFT scored 10/10 or 100%. Therefore, MSFT is definitely worth considering as a potential investment.

Is MSFT currently selling at a bargain price?

  1. Current Price Earnings less than Average historical PE Ratio? YES (25 < 32)
  2. Estimated Value is greater than Current Stock Price? YES (Value $450.12 > $373 Stock Price)
  3. Analysts’ Future Growth Forecasts greater than Current Stock Price? NO (Growth Forecasts $293 - $317 < $373 Stock Price)

Valuation metrics suggest that MSFT might be selling at a bargain price as indicated by the Estimated Value and PE Ratio. But the Future Growth Forecasts indicate that the share price could dip lower.

For me, the choice is certain. Microsoft is an excellent company to hold onto long-term, especially when it is selling at a fair price. I have recently taken a position in this company, and I plan to hold it with no intention of selling for a long time.

data.title: Central Bank Gold Statistics: Central Banks Stay The Course On Gold In February
data.description:
Central banks remain firm on gold accumulation in February, having bought a net 19t. This was a rebound from the lull we saw in January, though lower than the monthly average of 26t reported in 2025. Just two months into the year, central banks have bought 25t, a pace much slower than the same period last year (50t).

Chart 1: Central banks buying firm up in February

Monthly reported central banks activity, tonnes*

Reported activity in February was concentrated in:

  • The National Bank of Poland drove much of this month’s buying activity, having bought 20t. This brings its total gold reserves to 570t, lifting its share of total reserves to 31%. The bank has set a target of 700t of gold, as announced by its Governor, Adam Glapiński. In a recent development, Governor Glapiński had also proposed generating US$13bn through potential sale of gold reserves to finance defense spending, with the intention to “generate profits and to then buy it back”. Details on this proposal, though, remain unclear.
  • Central Bank of Uzbekistan bought 8t of gold this month, lifting its gold reserves to 407t or 88% of its total reserves. Y-t-d, Uzbekistan bought 16t so far this year.
  • Bank Negara Malaysia’s renewed interest continued into February, its second month of net purchases, adding 2t during the month. Y-t-d net purchases total a modest 5t. Czech National Bank’s modest but consistent net purchases bring its gold reserves to 75t or 7% of total reserves. A similar story for the People’s Bank of China - its 16th consecutive month of buying saw gold reserves grow to 2,308t or 10% of its total reserves.
  • Turkey (8t) and Russia (6t) saw the biggest declines in gold reserves in February. In Turkey’s case, the reduction appeared to be reflective of a decline in Treasury holdings rather than central bank reserves, based on our calculations. In March, however, the central bank was highly active, with our estimates indicating it utilised around 50t of its gold reserves for liquidity purposes and FX operations.1 Governor Fatih Karahan noted that: “a significant part of these transactions are in the nature of gold-currency swap futures. In other words, when it matures, the gold in question will return to our reserves.”2

Chart 2: Year-to-date central bank gold activity

Central bank net purchases and sales, tonnes*

African central banks look towards gold for diversification

The Bank of Uganda launched its domestic gold buying programme two years ago, with active buying commencing in March 2026. The bank aims to purchase at least 100kg of gold between March and June this year from artisanal, medium and large-scale domestic producers. The move is aimed to bolster reserves and cushion the economy from risks in international financial markets. Uganda is not alone in this shift. Kenya's central bank Governor Kamau Thugge signalled similar intentions at a news conference in early February, which alludes to a broader trend of African central banks turning to gold as a strategic diversification tool.

Conclusion

February seems to indicate a rebound in central bank buying after a quiet January, highlighting commitment to gold’s role in reserves. At the same time, central banks may be prudently price sensitive in their accumulation. Poland's contribution this month highlights continued appetite from established buyers, with persistent buying streaks from Czech Republic, China and Uzbekistan signalling sustained demand. New entrants from Southeast Asian and African central banks suggest that the emerging market story continues.

Footnotes

1Based on data to 20 March 2026.

2Statements from CBRT Governor Karahan on gold-based transactions, Bloomberg HT, March 2026.

data.title: What An Oil Shock Means For Monetary Policy
data.description:
Transcript

Eric Winograd - It’s still very early days in assessing the impact of the war in Iran on the global economy and on financial markets. There’s a lot more that we don’t know than there is that we do know. But it is far enough along for us to say that there will be an impact.

The transmission mechanism between that conflict and the global economy is likely to be through oil prices. While the move in oil prices has been large this time, it isn’t unprecedented. Certainly, we have seen bigger moves in energy prices as recently as the initial stages of the Russian invasion of Ukraine.

A good rule of thumb is that for every 10% increase in the price of oil in the US, that feeds through to headline inflation to the tune of about 0.2 percentage points. That’s meaningful, for sure, but it isn’t a paradigm shift.

It’s important to remember in that context, though, that it isn’t only inflation that is subject to change as a result of energy prices. Rising gasoline prices are essentially a tax on households and a tax on businesses, and the net result usually is slower growth. You have businesses and households having to spend more on fuel and less on more productive goods and services, and that slows the economy.

That combination of higher prices and lower growth is why we often talk about energy price shocks as being stagflationary. It’s not clear what the policy response to a stagflationary shock is supposed to be.

To the extent that central banks respond to higher prices, they would respond with tighter policy. This is the history in Europe, for example, where the European Central Bank has a single mandate to fight inflation and tends to raise rates in the face of supply shocks like this one. Certainly, the markets are pricing a significant probability that the ECB does raise rates over the next few months.

But the Fed has a dual mandate where they are required to focus not only on inflation but also on growth and on the labor market. And as the conflict drags on, if it does, the impact on growth is likely to get greater, and that puts the Fed in a difficult spot. If they’re going to respond to inflation, they should be tighter. If they’re going to respond to slower growth, they should be easier. And to us, that’s a recipe for policy stasis in the near term and probably even in the medium term. We think the Fed is likely to wait and see whether the inflationary impact or the growth impact is greater.

From a financial market perspective, stagflationary shocks like an oil supply disruption are difficult to manage. It’s not clear if markets should respond more to the potentially inflationary aspect of it or more to the growth slowdown aspect of it.

In the near term, what that has led to more than anything else is volatility. We’ve seen interest rates go up. We’ve seen equity markets go down. We think some of those moves are overdone at this point. The market is pricing a greater probability of a rate hike than we believe is likely, and indeed, our forecasts still have the Fed cutting rates over the course of the next 18 months or so.

data.description:

The Setup Advisors Should Be Paying Attention To

We don't often get a moment where the rate environment, the yield curve, and credit quality all line up at the same time. Right now, with long municipal bonds, that's exactly what's happening.

While the rate path remains uncertain with a new Fed leadership transition underway, the broader direction of the cycle and market expectations for eventual easing continue to favor long-duration fixed income. Additionally, muni supply is running hot. Cities and states issued a record near $600 billion in bonds last year, and 2026 is on pace to top that, driven by aging infrastructure needs and a surge in power sector demand tied to AI buildout. More supply means upward pressure on muni yields, even as the broader rate environment shifts. That combination is rare, and it's creating real value in the long end of the curve.

Muni Issuance Hits Record High in 2025

The Numbers Are Hard to Ignore

At roughly 4.5% nominal, 30-year AAA munis are translating to approximately 6.9–7.0% taxable equivalent yield for investors in the 35% bracket. That puts them about 140–150 basis points above comparable long corporates, and nearly 200 basis points over Treasuries. That kind of spread is the type of excess income you typically only see during periods of market stress. The difference here is that this isn't a credit dislocation story. It's being driven by rates, which may present a more favorable risk/reward profile relative to historical conditions.

The Muni Curve Is Doing Something Worth Watching

The slope of the muni curve is equally compelling. The spread between 10- and 30-year AAA munis sits at roughly +140 basis points nominal, or around 220 basis points on a taxable-equivalent basis. That's a notably steep configuration by historical standards, and it means investors are genuinely getting paid to extend duration: not just in carry, but in convexity and roll-down benefits as well.

AAA Yield Curve as of 03/30/26

For clients in the 37% bracket, the tax-exempt nature of muni income continues to be one of the most efficient tools available. With taxable yields under pressure, the after-tax comparison versus corporates or Treasuries is as favorable as it's been in a while.

Why This Isn't a Reach-for-Yield Trade

One thing worth clarifying for advisors: this is not a credit story dressed up as a value opportunity. Long high-grade municipals are competing directly with corporate credit on income while carrying higher credit quality. The valuation case is built on rate dynamics, not on moving down the credit ladder to find yield.

That distinction matters. It means the forward-looking risk/reward isn't dependent on credit conditions holding up. It's dependent on a rate environment that, directionally, is moving the right way.

Why MLN, and Why Now

Part of what makes this moment interesting is that long munis have had less institutional attention over the past decade. Banks and insurance companies have largely moved away from 30-year commitments, and SMA structures have shifted toward intermediate bonds in response to curve steepness and lower volatility. That rotation has left the long end of the muni market relatively under-owned, which only adds to the opportunity.

Historically, moving earlier in a rate easing cycle has allowed investors to participate more fully in potential price appreciation in long-duration fixed income (although outcomes can vary and are not guaranteed).. Waiting for the move to be obvious usually means the repricing has already happened.

How to Access Long-Duration Munis

Advisors looking to position clients for this setup can access it through the VanEck Long Munis ETF (MLN), which provides targeted exposure to long-dated investment-grade municipal bonds and is built to reflect the opportunity set that exists at the long end of the muni market today.

data.title: Mobileye: Stuck In Neutral
data.description:
Mobileye Global Inc. (MBLY) is still struggling to transition from a leader in advanced driver assistance features for vehicles to fully autonomous driving. A prime issue is that the market has shifted to where passenger vehicles haven't moved forward with autonomous driving goals at the same speed as robotaxis, where Mobileye isn't as heavily involved. My investment thesis remains Neutral on the stock, looking for an inflection point in the market.

Robotaxi Struggles

Mobileye is the global leader in technology and software for advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS), primarily focused on the EyeQ SoC offering. The company generally operates up to the L2 level while the market is looking to shift to L4 robotaxis.

The company has a massive 8-year future expected automotive revenue pipeline of $24.5 billion, up 42% over the last couple of years. The pipeline amounts to ~$3 billion in annual revenue over the period compared to current annual revenues of nearly $2 billion.

Mobileye ships about 35 million EyeQ chips on an annual basis, but the issue is the transition from base ADAS services to AVs. The robotaxi world is moving full-speed ahead with Waymo, owned by Google (GOOG), WeRide (WRD), and potentially Tesla (TSLA), with Mobileye generally excluded from participating with the big players in this market.

The company has announced multiple deals with AV programs, but most of these deals aren't with market leading players. Moia, owned by Volkswagen Group (VWAGY), is working on a robotaxi in the Los Angeles market, where Waymo has fully operated since November 2024.

Mobileye likes to point out that Volkswagen Autonomous Mobility has plans to offer robotaxi services across 6 cities by the end of 2027 and target a fleet of 100,000 vehicles by 2033. Again, Mobileye has promising opportunities in the robotaxi space, but the technology and partners aren't seen as market leaders, and one really has to wonder if Moia has room for a large market position up to 7 years from now with the scaling of Waymo and potential size of Tesla with the Cybercab ready to start production of a $30K robotaxi this month.

Dead Money

The stock continues slumping to new all-time lows due to the precarious position of the business. The Robotaxi market is full-speed ahead, and Mobileye guided to 2026 revenues of flat to only up 5%.

The company has been stuck around the $2 billion sales levels for years now. The stock has a market cap of just $6 billion, or only 3x sales, due to this tepid outlook for AVs.

Berenberg just upgraded the stock with a $9.30 price target. Mobileye shareholders have mostly blown off the call due to the realization the stock started the year above $10, a higher level than this bullish call.

Mobileye is only forecast to produce an adjusted operating income of ~$200 million this year. The stock trades at about 30x this target, as the company is no longer highly profitable due to the need to invest in the business.

The company recently announced a deal to acquire Mentee Robotics to enter the humanoid robotics space. The deal has a cost of $900 million, comprising ~$612 million in cash and up to 26.2 million shares.

The humanoid robotics space is highly competitive, and Mobileye will need to invest heavily in a business competing with the likes of Tesla and a ton of Chinese operators. Musk has long announced plans to ramp up Optimus production to 1 million units, and Mobileye lacks the capital to compete in this space with better capitalized players.

Mobileye has a strong balance sheet with $1.84 billion in cash to end 2025. The Mentee Robotics deal will cut the cash balance to $1.23 billion, which is still a solid cash balance, but Mobileye has seen operating profits slip, and Mentee Robotics will boost operating expenses, probably materially, to have any real hope for success.

The stock isn't necessarily expensive now, trading at under 20x 2027 EPS targets of $0.38. The concern is Mobileye being dead money unless the robotaxi business ramps up, or passenger vehicle customers finally shift towards AV technologies.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Mobileye now operates in a couple of interesting categories, autonomous vehicle technology and humanoid robots, but the company is not a leader in either segment. Investors should continue watching the stock from the sidelines, looking for any competitive update suggesting a technology inflection is taking place to where the company will thrive in the ongoing technology shift. Otherwise, Mobileye will continue drifting lower.
